speaker: Jenny Wen
text: This design process that designers have been taught, where you go off and do a bunch of research and discovery, and then you diverge, converge, diverge, converge — we sort of treated as gospel. That's basically dead.
summary: The traditional design process of diverge-converge cycles that designers treated as gospel is now dead in the age of AI.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: There are basically two types of design work now. The first is supporting implementation and execution — engineers are using their seven Claudes to create features. The second is creating the vision or direction, but now it's a three to six month vision, not a two to five year one, and it's sometimes just creating a prototype that points people in the right direction.
summary: Design work has stratified into two modes: supporting fast engineering execution and setting short-term vision through prototypes rather than beautiful long-term decks.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: It's not just designers who are feeling like, 'Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers.' I think even engineers are like, 'How do we keep up with ourselves?'
summary: The pace of AI-driven engineering change is so fast that even engineers are struggling to keep up with their own capabilities.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: The way you really lose trust around quality and releasing something early is if you release it early and then nothing ever happens. But whenever you put something out early, it's possible to maintain the brand of your company if you commit to iterating, responding to feedback, and continuously shipping improvements.
summary: Shipping early is fine for brand trust as long as you visibly iterate and respond to user feedback — what kills trust is releasing something early and then going silent.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: I think AI will get better at taste and judgment and design. We might be holding onto that a little bit too much. At the end of the day, someone has to decide what is actually going to get built and what actually matters. Someone still needs to be accountable for the decision.
summary: AI will improve at taste and design judgment, but humans will still be needed for accountability and deciding what actually matters to build.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: Being an IC across this past year gave me so many hard skills I wouldn't have gained if I was just managing. The design process has changed so much that I think design managers need to move back into IC work to truly understand what is happening, so they can be better managers.
summary: Design managers should rotate back into IC work to build empathy and hard skills for the rapidly changing AI-era design process.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: You have to know the difference between a megatrend and a hype cycle. When there's a megatrend, don't fight it. AI is a megatrend, one of the most foundational movements that we have seen in human history.
summary: Leaders must distinguish megatrends from hype cycles — AI is a megatrend you should never fight.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Every management book says praise in public, criticize in private. I fundamentally disagree. Establish enough trust so you are comfortable critiquing and debating in public. In private, build that trust. In public, you don't want posturing — you want problem-solving.
summary: Reverse the conventional wisdom: critique and debate in public for problem-solving, build trust and reassurance in private.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Don't delegate the storytelling. The story is not a marketing exercise after you built the product. The story is why you build the product. If you have seven or eight layers between you and the front line, you can't play the telephone game. Always own telling the story yourself.
summary: CEOs should never delegate company storytelling — the story defines why you build, and telephone-game message loss across org layers guarantees failure.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: The six things you need to build a great company, in descending order: timing, market, team, product, brand, distribution. You don't have all six, you don't win. A great team in a bad market gets dragged down — the market always wins.
summary: Great companies need six elements in priority order: timing, market, team, product, brand, and distribution — the market always trumps the team.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Permission to play means asking: is this logical for us? Do we have the route to market for mass scale distribution? If you dissipate your caloric burn across too many areas, nothing gets enough girth to drive all the way through. Be extremely selective where you expend your calories.
summary: Companies should only enter markets where they have permission to play and a distribution advantage.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Persistence beats intellect, and stamina beats intellect any day of the week, twice on Sunday. The biggest reason successful leaders win is not that they're the smartest — it's that they have enormous staying power in the game.
summary: Stamina and persistence matter more than intelligence for leadership success.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: The platform that you choose and the quality of problems that you pick to solve determine a lot of the path of success for you. Harder problems attract better people, and business is a team sport.
summary: Choose hard problems to attract the best talent — easy problems won't draw the team you need to win.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 30 pull requests.
summary: Boris has fully transitioned to AI-written code, shipping 10-30 PRs daily without manual editing.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: Productivity per engineer has increased 200%. I have never enjoyed coding as much as I do today, because I don't have to deal with all the minutia.
summary: 200% productivity increase from AI coding tools, with higher job satisfaction due to reduced tedious work.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: In a year or two, it's not going to matter. Coding is virtually solved. I imagine a world where everyone is able to program, anyone can just build software any time.
summary: Predicts coding will be virtually solved in 1-2 years, enabling universal software creation.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: Claude is starting to come up with ideas. It's looking for feedback, it's looking at bug reports, it's looking at telemetry for bug fixes, and things to ship. A little more like a coworker.
summary: AI coding agents are evolving from tools to autonomous coworkers that identify and ship improvements independently.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: I look for four things in CEOs — my LOCK algorithm. L is for lovable — can I envision following this person? O is obsession with the problem. C is chip on the shoulder. K is deeply knowledgeable about the domain. And I'd add S for student — the best ones are deep, deep students of the game.
summary: The LOCKS framework for evaluating CEOs: Lovable, Obsessed, Chip-on-shoulder, Knowledgeable, and Student of the game.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: Starting a company has never been easier. The flip side is the number of companies formed is going to mushroom. There's so much noise and competition, it's just going to be hard to stand out and really accelerate and scale. It's never been easier to start, never been harder to scale.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: If you want to kill a plant, have two people water it. Every CEO at the adults' table has gone through this and they are religious about the DRI — directly responsible individual.
summary: Shared ownership kills execution at scale — every important initiative needs a single DRI.

text: EV is enterprise value, TV is your team's value, MEV is your value. Immature managers solve for TV over EV. We always put on the wall: solve for CV, then EV, then TV, then MEV. Customers first, then company, then team, then yourself.
summary: The priority hierarchy for decisions should be Customer Value, Enterprise Value, Team Value, then My Value.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: I always valued optionality, but there's a massive tax in optionality when you can move this fast. Planning cycles used to be a year, now they're three months.
summary: In the AI era, optionality is expensive — planning cycles have shrunk from annual to quarterly.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: The go-to-market is going to get turned on its head. People start evaluating products in Gemini or ChatGPT. Your website is a lot less important. Sites will change to have a high quality avatar that knows everything about your products.
summary: Go-to-market will be transformed as buyers research in AI chatbots instead of websites.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: IC engineers are becoming tech leads. They're managing fleets and fleets of agents. I know many engineers on my team have 10 to 20 threads being pulled on at the same time.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The Mythical Man-Month predicted software engineering would go where engineers are like surgeons. As a manager, look around corners and unblock people, especially from organizational bottlenecks.
summary: Managers should treat engineers like surgeons — proactively removing organizational blockers.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast. Build for where the models are going, not where they are today.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: We live in a bubble in Silicon Valley. A lot of work that runs our entire economy is not open-ended knowledge work — it's repeatable business processes with standard operating procedures.
summary: Business process automation is a massively underrated AI opportunity.

text: Every startup that I've seen fizzle out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come and squashed them. It's because they built something that didn't resonate with customers.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: The market is immutable. No amount of tweeting, advertising is going to change whether the market wants your product.
summary: Product-market fit is like drug receptor binding — the market wants your product or it does not.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: Point solutions don't have enough data in the age of AI to be useful. If you own the mine with the data, you can make money.
summary: In the AI era, point SaaS solutions lack sufficient data context — only companies owning first-party data capture value.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: The purest form of ambition and most intense source of energy in the business is the founder CEO. Every next concentric circle of management has the potential to be an order of magnitude drop off in intensity.

speaker: Elena Verna
text: The best growth teams I've seen all have one thing in common: they measure retention before acquisition. If your bucket is leaky, pouring more water in doesn't help.

speaker: Elena Verna

summary: Good gamification makes progress visible through mechanics like leaderboards and streaks, not superficial badges.

speaker: Elena Verna

speaker: Edwin Chen

speaker: Edwin Chen

text: There's actually three levels to listening. Level one is internal. Level two is focused. Level three is global listening — you hear what they're communicating, not just saying.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: I use a GROW model. G is goal, R is reality, O is options, W is way forward.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: We operate in tech. We're supposed to give all of ourselves purely logically. That's not at all true. The goal of any conflict is to create mutual understanding.

speaker: Rachel Lockett

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: Would you enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role? That's the question we always asked at Stripe.

summary: Founders must create both the product and the market, positioning through familiar concepts.

speaker: Shaun Clowes
text: In everything always talk from the customer's perspective, from the market's perspective, from the competitor's perspective. The very small number of PMs do that. They get dragged into internal politics, scrum management.
summary: The best PMs consistently frame everything from customer, market, and competitor perspectives rather than getting dragged into internal politics and scrum management.

speaker: Shaun Clowes
text: The way AI will most impact product management is data management. If you don't have your data house in order, your AI products will be garbage. Data is the moat, not the model.
summary: AI's biggest impact on product management will be through data management because the quality of your data, not your model, determines product quality.

speaker: Shaun Clowes
text: If we were doctors, you'd be like, 'That's totally unacceptable.' Imagine if after 20 years of medical practice, most doctors still couldn't reliably diagnose common conditions.
summary: The low bar for PM competency would be unacceptable in fields like medicine, highlighting how much the discipline still needs to mature.

text: I'm throwing everything against the wall. I'm looking at what's going to stick. I am looking to try to find the winning tactic and turn it into a winning strategy.
summary: Benioff's approach to launching Salesforce was relentless experimentation, throwing everything at the wall and turning winning tactics into strategy.

summary: Thawar managed 120 direct reports by replacing traditional management with systems like pair programming and product backlogs for unblocking.

summary: Thawar's life motto 'everything you know is wrong' drives constant experimentation and first-principles thinking.

text: Test everything. We've run in the last four years over 600 experiments on the streaks, so every other day. We've actually set up really good infrastructure for copy testing.
summary: Duolingo runs an experiment on streaks every other day, totaling 600+ experiments in four years, with dedicated copy testing infrastructure.

summary: Changing a single CTA from 'continue' to 'commit to my goal' was a massive win, demonstrating the power of psychologically resonant copy.

speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth

summary: Duolingo frames its core challenge as habit formation, not language teaching, with next-day retention as the most important metric.

speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth
text: Loss aversion is way more powerful than reward seeking. The fear of losing your streak is more motivating than any badge or reward we could give you.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: AI very soon is going to stop being a feature the same way electricity is not a feature. What AI companies and all companies need to do is say, what's in this for the user? What promise do I want to make, a difficult promise, a remarkable promise, and then how do I keep it?
summary: AI will stop being a differentiating feature like electricity stopped being one; companies must focus on the promise they make to users, not the technology.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: I define good taste as knowing what other people want just before they do. People are seen as having good taste when they bring something to the world that the world didn't necessarily expect but is glad to see.
summary: Good taste is the ability to anticipate what people want before they know it themselves, delivering the pleasantly unexpected.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: Quality is not luxury. Quality is not perfection. Quality means meeting spec, and if you meet spec, you're done. If you don't think the spec is good enough, make a better spec.
summary: Quality means meeting spec, not perfection; if the spec feels insufficient, the answer is to raise the spec, not to endlessly polish.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: If you don't build the network effect into what you are making, you are almost certainly going to fail. The question is, will this work better for my users if they tell other people about it?
summary: Software products that do not have network effects built in from the start are almost certainly going to fail.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: When you choose your smallest viable audience, what language they speak, how much money they have, what problem they're trying to solve, you have chosen everything that's going to go into the product and what your future is going to be like.
summary: Choosing your smallest viable audience is the most consequential product decision because it determines everything about what you build.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: I have a very emotional connection to Claude.ai. I think they have a brand. ChatGPT's reputation with me is not good because it regularly over promises and under delivers and it does it without kindness or humility.
summary: Godin sees Claude as having a genuine brand built on kindness and humility, while ChatGPT over-promises and under-delivers.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: To be a founder is a state of being, it's an attitude. To be a CEO is a craft. The more founders who can accept that those are two separate things and they're both equally important to build an ascendant startup, the better all of us will be.
summary: Being a founder is a mindset while being a CEO is a learnable craft, and recognizing these as separate skills is crucial for startup success.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: Founder mode gets me angry. That article just got me hot. It really felt like an excuse. We were giving founders permission to not learn the job.
summary: Lowenhar sees the 'founder mode' concept as a dangerous excuse that gives founders permission to avoid learning the craft of being CEO.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: There are these two phases to a startup journey. Phase one is finding product-market fit. Phase two is building the company. Most founders focus entirely on phase one and never learn how to do phase two.
summary: Most founders obsess over finding product-market fit but never learn the equally critical second phase of actually building the company.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: The greatest CEOs know when to calibrate which one is needed. It's not manager mode is bad, it's that you need both modes and you need to know when to deploy each.
summary: Great CEOs do not choose between founder mode and manager mode; they calibrate between both based on what the situation demands.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: The founders who scale are the ones who realize that their job description changes every six months. What got you here won't get you there, and that's not a failure, it's the nature of the journey.
summary: Founders who scale successfully accept that their job description fundamentally changes every six months as the company grows.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: The idea behind Replit is that making software today is very difficult. We want to make it easier. People view this as a developer in their pocket essentially. We have 34 million users globally.
summary: Replit's mission is to democratize software creation, serving as a developer-in-your-pocket for 34 million global users.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: Typically, you're bottlenecked where your ideas are not fitting in because they need to be made and they need to be made quickly. Now, you open up that bottleneck. So now actually making things is a lot easier. Actually, you become limited by how fast you can generate ideas.
summary: AI removes the implementation bottleneck in product development, shifting the constraint from building speed to idea generation speed.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: I could imagine whatever five years from now, someone running a billion-dollar company with zero employees where it's like the support is handled by AI, the development is handled by AI, and you're just building and creating this thing.
summary: Masad envisions billion-dollar companies run by a single person within five years, with AI handling support, development, and operations.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: The evals are everything. You can't ship an AI feature without a way to measure whether it's actually good. We built an entire internal evaluation infrastructure before we shipped our agent.
summary: Replit built comprehensive internal evaluation infrastructure before shipping their AI agent because evals are the foundation of AI product quality.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: The best way to build an AI product is to use it yourself constantly. Our team uses Replit Agent to build Replit. That feedback loop is the fastest way to improve.
summary: Replit uses its own AI agent to build Replit itself, creating the tightest possible feedback loop for product improvement.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg
text: I was hired as the first product manager. I sat in on those calls. I still did not exactly understand what we were going to build, which was confusing. And that ended up pivoting us around to cloud security.
summary: Wiz's first PM joined before the product idea was figured out, and her confusion about what to build actually catalyzed the pivot to cloud security.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg
text: We would have 10 to 15 meetings every day with potential customers. At the time, we didn't really have a solid product yet.
summary: Wiz conducted 10-15 customer meetings daily before having a product, using extreme customer proximity to find product-market fit.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg
text: We went from zero to $100 million ARR faster than any SaaS company in history. But it wasn't luck. It was the combination of a team that had built and sold a company together before, and a market that was screaming for a solution.
summary: Wiz's record-breaking growth from zero to $100M ARR came from a team with prior exit experience targeting a market with extreme urgency.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg

summary: Wiz deliberately sold to CISOs rather than security engineers because executive buyers commit to deployments, not pilots.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg
text: Our product strategy is simple: we want to be the operating system for cloud security. Every new product we build has to connect to the graph. That's our moat.
summary: Wiz's moat is its connected security graph, and every new product must integrate into it, creating compounding value.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: When you have teams naturally break up the world into different funnel stages, it gets very seductive to look at my part of the funnel and what's my conversion rate. summary: Funnel-stage teams are tempted to optimize their conversion rate by making the prior step harder, which is counterproductive to overall growth.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: The best way to get more people to get to a step is just get more people in the door in the first place. That will always hurt your conversion rate, but it may actually give you more people on the outside.
summary: Widening the funnel top hurts conversion rates but produces more absolute outcomes, which matters more than percentages.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: Shopify doesn't have KPIs in the traditional sense. We have a north star which is GMS, gross merchandise sales, and everything else is context. We trust people to make good decisions.
summary: Shopify avoids traditional KPIs in favor of a single north star metric (GMS) and trusts teams to make decisions with context.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: We actually optimize for churn. Not to reduce it to zero, but to make sure we're acquiring the right merchants. If we reduce churn by making it hard to leave, we're just trapping bad-fit customers.
summary: Shopify optimizes for healthy churn rather than minimizing it, because trapping bad-fit merchants with exit barriers hurts long-term growth.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: We build toward a 100-year vision. That sounds abstract but it changes every decision. You stop optimizing for this quarter's numbers and start building things that compound.
summary: Shopify's 100-year vision changes daily decisions by shifting focus from quarterly optimization to building compounding value.

text: Our jobs get frustrating when we behave, most of the time, in misalignment with our superpowers and who we truly are at our core. I have a preferred level at which I like to operate.
summary: Career frustration stems from spending most of your time operating at a level misaligned with your core superpowers and natural strengths.

speaker: Shreyas Doshi

summary: Pre-mortems surface hidden risks by asking teams to imagine project failure before starting, unlocking candid discussion of concerns.

speaker: Shreyas Doshi
text: The LNO framework: categorize every task as Leverage, Neutral, or Overhead. Do leverage tasks at 100%, neutral at 80%, and overhead at the minimum acceptable quality.
summary: The LNO framework categorizes tasks as Leverage, Neutral, or Overhead, with effort allocated proportionally to each category's impact.

speaker: Shreyas Doshi
text: Product work happens at three levels: impact, execution, and optics. Most execution problems are actually strategy problems. When you can't get alignment, it's because there's no clear strategy, not because people aren't executing.

summary: Impact-effort matrices create false rigor because truly high-impact work is almost never low-effort, making the framework misleading.

speaker: Shreyas Doshi
text: Thinking is cheap, so you should do more thinking, not less. The bias toward action in tech is overdone. Sometimes the highest leverage thing you can do is sit and think for two days before making a decision.

speaker: Anneka Gupta

summary: True strategic leaders combine articulating a clear vision with the courage to champion difficult changes -- one without the other falls short.

speaker: Tristan de Montebello
text: The games are meant to put you in a state of turbulence and find out what was easy, what was hard, what am I noticing? summary: Meaningful practice in public speaking comes from reps that push you to an edge of discomfort -- perfect reps teach you nothing.

speaker: Tristan de Montebello
text: It's so much more freeing, powerful, connecting, and effective to speak conversation.
summary: Conversational speaking is more powerful and connecting than polished presentation style -- it frees both the speaker and the audience.

text: Get clear on your objective function, and one way that I've gotten clear on it is trying to think about it from future me because five years from now, I'm not going to give a if I made the presentation slightly better, but I'm going to care a lot about what kind of relationship I have with my daughters.
summary: Clarify your life's objective function by asking what future-you will actually care about -- it reframes daily priorities away from trivial work tasks.

speaker: John Mark Nickels
text: Not to be morbid, but most of us just aren't really tuned into an awareness that our lives will come to an end. We try to pretend like we're going to live forever and just not think about it. summary: Accepting mortality is not morbid but clarifying -- it forces a genuine reprioritization of what actually matters in work and life.

speaker: Alex Komoroske
text: We've forgotten a world without aggregators. Aggregators make sense in the late stage of an era. At the beginning, they curtail too much exploration.

speaker: Alex Komoroske
text: Software is alchemy. It's the ability to extend human agency beyond ourselves to create something that can then combine with what others have created in unexpected and unforeseen ways. And somehow in the past decade, we've become convinced that all of this potential should be squeezed into about a dozen little boxes on your phone.
summary: Software should extend human agency and combine with others' creations in unforeseen ways, not be trapped inside monolithic apps controlled by a few companies.

speaker: Alex Komoroske
text: Do things that give you energy that you're proud of, and happiness is reality minus expectations I think are really simple clarifying words.
summary: Two clarifying life principles: pursue work that gives you energy and pride, and remember that happiness equals reality minus expectations.

speaker: Benjamin Lauzier
text: I think when you're running a marketplace, you tend to sit in your ivory tower a little bit, looking at stats and thinking like, 'If only we could get people to do X, it'd be better for everyone.' I think that's missing the point that we're humans.

speaker: Benjamin Lauzier
text: I'm a huge believer in market forces and empowerment, so provide guardrails for what a good experience is in your marketplace, set a clear bar for quality, and provide the right coaching and tools for supply to be successful, and then take a step back and see where the gaps are.

text: Make sure you go somewhere where you have a good engineering partner. Because if you have great ideas of what to build but you can't get them built, then you go nowhere. So that has to be part of your evaluation criteria that you meet and value your engineering partner before you join.

speaker: Eli Schwartz
text: Think of SEO as a product. The product managers are the people that should be thinking about this SEO question because it's a product question. summary: SEO should be treated as a product problem, not a marketing trick -- PMs should own it because it is about serving users on a self-discovery journey.

speaker: Eli Schwartz
text: Transparently, I thought this was going to be an apocalypse. Up until AI Overviews, whoever won on that long form piece of content would get that first click. But now that doesn't exist anymore.
summary: AI Overviews in search have fundamentally changed SEO by eliminating the first-click advantage that long-form content used to enjoy.

speaker: Eli Schwartz
text: In their documentation, they say that AI itself is not the problem. It's the helpfulness, the usefulness of the content that would be a problem.
summary: Google's stance on AI content is that AI authorship itself is not penalized -- what matters is whether the content is genuinely useful to users.

speaker: Camille Fournier
text: Hoarding credit. PMs, they tend to be the front-facing person for initiative. Engineers sometimes think that they don't get the credit for their work because the PM takes all the glory and all the credit for the project that they really worked very hard on.
summary: The number one thing PMs do that annoys engineers is hoarding credit -- being the front-facing person while engineers feel invisible for the real work.

speaker: Camille Fournier
text: The next thing that engineers really get annoyed about with PMs, when they just don't understand the details and act like they don't matter, it just shows a real lack of empathy for the work that engineers are doing and I think it really can be very off-putting.
summary: PMs who dismiss technical details signal a lack of empathy for engineering work, which is deeply off-putting to their engineering partners.

speaker: Phyl Terry
text: When you're looking for a job, you need a spear and not a net. What happens when we're building a product? Same thing, right? We want this product to be for everyone, but we've learned with product market fit that doesn't work. We need a narrow, clear focus.
summary: Job searching requires a spear, not a net -- just like product-market fit, you need narrow focus rather than trying to appeal to everyone.

speaker: Phyl Terry
text: While it's hard to figure out your candidate market fit, it's also a relief to know it's not about you. So what I ask people to do is I ask them to think about what they want and what they don't want. Now, you might not think that that's a radical step, but most people don't do that. When they get laid off, they spray and pray.
summary: Most people skip the fundamental step of defining what they want and do not want in a job -- instead they spray and pray, which rarely works.

speaker: Phyl Terry
text: There's no I in team. Well, there is an I in village, and the I in village is that when you start to interview and negotiate, you've got to be in charge. I want you to play to win, not not to lose.
summary: In job negotiations, shift from playing not-to-lose to playing to win -- take charge of the interview and negotiation process.

speaker: Sean Ellis
text: The question is, how would you feel if you could no longer use this product? Once you got a high enough percentage of users saying they'd be very disappointed, most of those products did pretty well. If you felt too low, those products tended to suffer.
summary: The Sean Ellis test -- asking users how disappointed they would be without the product -- reliably predicts product-market fit when 40%+ say very disappointed.

speaker: Sean Ellis
text: Just ignore the people who say they'd be somewhat disappointed. They're telling you it's a nice to have. summary: Focus on 'very disappointed' users, not 'somewhat disappointed' ones -- optimizing for nice-to-have feedback risks diluting the experience for your core fans.

speaker: Sean Ellis
text: It's usually much more function of onboarding to the right user experience than it is about the tactical things that people try to do to improve retention.
summary: Retention is driven more by onboarding users to the right experience than by tactical retention hacks.

speaker: Sean Ellis

text: How do you start a fire? Well, you start it by putting a little kindling, little crumpled up paper, and you hold the match in one place until the fire starts. summary: Crossing the chasm requires holding the match in one place with adjacent kindling, meaning dominating one tight beachhead before expanding to adjacent segments.

speaker: Geoffrey Moore
text: So how do you start a fire? Well, you start it by putting a little kindling, little crumpled up paper, and you hold the match in one place until the fire starts.
summary: Market domination begins with concentrated effort on a narrow segment that can catch fire and spread to adjacent markets.

speaker: Richard Rumelt
text: Don't call it strategy, call it an action agenda. Begin to try to identify the one or two key challenges that can actually be addressed and what are we going to do about it? What are the coherent actions we're going to do to take these on?
summary: Real strategy is simply identifying the one or two key addressable challenges and defining coherent actions to tackle them, not crafting mission and vision statements.

speaker: Richard Rumelt
text: It's huge numbers of people out there willing to sell you advice on mission and your vision and your values, all these things that have to be in place before you can have strategy. That's not true.
summary: The strategy industry has created a false prerequisite that you need mission, vision, and values before you can think strategically.

speaker: Richard Rumelt
text: We're going to go after this and here's the action steps we're going to take to do that. That's the essence of what you're doing when you're thinking strategically.
summary: Strategic thinking at its core is choosing what to go after and defining the specific steps to get there.

speaker: Heidi Helfand
text: Reteaming is hard. Reorgs are hard. You can't lump them all into one thing with oh, it's all great all the time. No, it's not. If we could just build the software, deliver to the customer, get the product market fit, hey, have we delighted them or not? If only it could be that easy. No, we have the people layer, so let's focus there too.
summary: Companies focus on product and market fit but often neglect the people layer, where reteaming and reorgs are unavoidable and deserve equal attention.

speaker: Heidi Helfand
text: Change and growth are natural parts of company evolution. Don't fear reteaming or reorgs; instead, embrace these changes, actively plan for them, and leverage the five reteaming patterns to create a dynamic and adaptive organizational culture.
summary: Rather than fearing reorgs, leaders should proactively plan for team changes using structured reteaming patterns.

speaker: Ethan Evans
text: People think invention takes all this time, but you only need two hours once a month. The thing is, once you have one good idea, it often takes years to express that.
summary: Innovation requires surprisingly little ideation time; the real work is the years of execution needed to fully express a single good idea.

speaker: Ethan Evans
text: Jeff and Amazon had ideas like, 'Let's have Prime shipping.' Prime is still getting better and still being worked on. It's a 20 some year old idea. The Kindle, a decades old idea now still getting better. The point here is you don't need very many good ideas to be seen as tremendously inventive.
summary: Amazon Prime and Kindle are decades-old ideas still being refined, proving that being inventive requires very few ideas but deep, sustained execution.

speaker: Ethan Evans
text: You had the idea to have a newsletter. You've been working on the expression of that idea for years now.
summary: Even Lenny's newsletter is an example of a single idea that took years to fully express and develop into a business.

speaker: Sam Schillace
text: We tend to undervalue the things we're good at. We tend to think work has to be unpleasant. And so if something is easy and fun, we don't tend to think it's valuable. You should go do the thing that you feel guilty to get paid for, if there's a thing like that, and do the hell out of it.
summary: People undervalue their natural talents because easy work feels illegitimate; the best career strategy is to maximize what feels effortless and fun.

speaker: Sam Schillace
text: Lots of people gravitate in this direction of like, let's go do unpleasant things and grind our way through the career because that's the way to make it. But the reality is... if you get pleasure from doing something that people want to pay you for, do it the best you can do it, as hard as you can do it. Work doesn't necessarily have to be hard.
summary: The 'grind through unpleasant work' mentality is a trap; the creator of Google Docs argues that leaning into what brings you joy creates the most value.

speaker: Sam Schillace
text: If that's messing around and playing around with cool ideas, do the hell out of that.
summary: Playful exploration and experimentation with ideas is a legitimate and often superior path to career success and innovation.

speaker: Will Larson
text: I think that we often treat engineers a little bit like children instead of giving them the responsibilities and ability to actually thrive as adults. 'Oh, the engineers won't want to do that work.' Well, that's actually not good for the engineers to be sheltered from what is important.
summary: Product organizations inadvertently stunt engineer growth by shielding them from real business problems instead of treating them as capable adults.

speaker: Will Larson
text: One of the highlights is that I think we're coming back this moment where we can actually treat engineers like our peers and put them into really senior leadership roles and not have this kind of baseline assumption of like, 'Oh, we have to coddle them or hide them from the real problems.'
summary: The industry is returning to a moment where engineers can be placed in senior leadership roles rather than being protected from business realities.

speaker: Will Larson
text: This is how they're going to get the opportunity to grow as well.
summary: Exposing engineers to real business problems is not just organizationally beneficial but essential for their personal career growth.

speaker: Judd Antin
text: User-centered performance refers to customer obsession or user-centered practice that is symbolic rather than focused on learning. It's hugely common. It's work we do to signal to each other how customer obsessed we are, not because we want to make a different decision.
summary: Much of what companies call 'user research' is actually performative -- done to signal customer obsession rather than to genuinely inform decisions.

speaker: Judd Antin
text: Every time a PM comes to a researcher at the end of a product process and says, 'Can you just run a quick user study just to validate our assumptions,' that's user-centered performance. It's too late to matter. We got to ship it. What they want is to check the box.
summary: Late-stage validation studies are the most common form of user-centered performance -- they exist to check a box, not to change a decision.

speaker: Judd Antin
text: One of my big mantras was, 'We don't validate, we falsify. We are looking to be wrong.' Many PMs, many designers are not in that place. They do not want to be wrong. They're looking to validate, and that's user-centered performance.
summary: True user research seeks to falsify assumptions rather than validate them, but most product teams are psychologically wired to confirm what they already believe.

speaker: Nir Eyal
text: I went to Alibaba and I bought myself one of these flip phones from China like we used to have in the 1990s with no apps, no internet connection. And then I got myself a word processor off of eBay so that I could just sit down and write. And even when I stopped using all the technology... I kept getting distracted because the problem is not our technology. The problem is our inability to deal with discomfort.
summary: Even after eliminating all technology, Nir Eyal still got distracted, proving that the root cause of distraction is emotional discomfort, not devices.

speaker: Nir Eyal
text: Always identify what is that internal trigger, what is that itch that you are looking to escape when you get distracted, because that is the source of 90% of our distractions.
summary: 90% of distraction stems from internal triggers -- emotional itches we try to escape -- not from external notifications or technology.

speaker: Nir Eyal
text: Time management is pain management. The reason we get distracted is not because of what's happening outside of us but what's happening inside of us.
summary: The core insight of Indistractable is that managing your time is really about managing your relationship with discomfort.

speaker: Sarah Tavel
text: I think a lot of people think about markets almost like these bodies of water, it's like it's this big body of water that we're going after. I actually think that the most interesting markets, you have to think of them like currents where there's something happening in the market that's creating this current where you can have a plank of wood that you've put on the river and it's going to pull you forward.
summary: The best markets are not static pools of opportunity but dynamic currents that pull companies forward, making market momentum more important than market size.

speaker: Sarah Tavel
text: That's why we care less about market size because really, what you're looking for when you're looking at a market, are what are the dynamics of change, what's the current and momentum that's going to pull the company and make the job easier for the founders to actually build something that endures.
summary: Benchmark cares less about TAM and more about the dynamics of change in a market, because momentum does the heavy lifting for founders.

speaker: Sarah Tavel
text: Versus a market that doesn't really have that momentum to it, you're going to have to build something really big and fancy to make any progress.
summary: In markets without natural momentum, even the best product requires enormous effort to make progress, while dynamic markets amplify modest efforts.

speaker: Christian Idiodi
text: I try to explain to people that the real essence of this job is that you wake up on behalf of someone else to solve a problem for them, and you have to do it well enough that they give you something back in return. That's kind of the real essence of it, and that's, I always call it a certificate of appreciation.
summary: Product management at its core is waking up to solve someone else's problem well enough that they reward you with revenue, engagement, or loyalty.

speaker: Christian Idiodi
text: If it's not fun, you're probably not doing it right. If it's not hard, you're probably also not doing it right.
summary: Good product management should be both fun and hard; if it's missing either quality, something is off about how you're approaching the role.

speaker: Christian Idiodi
text: And it could be in the form of revenue, engagement, loyalty, reference, all of those things.
summary: The 'certificate of appreciation' from customers manifests as revenue, engagement, loyalty, or referrals -- all signals that you've solved their problem well.

speaker: Jason Fried
text: The reason I think it's great for entrepreneurs to start bootstrapping is because they just have more practice making money, and they get better, and better, and better at the fundamental skill you need to have ultimately to run a successful business, which is to make money.
summary: Bootstrapping forces founders to practice the fundamental skill of making money from day one, while funded founders often defer this critical learning.

speaker: Jason Fried
text: Basically almost nobody makes it that way. Really, almost nobody really makes it that way, and there's a lot more room to make it, and to build a successful business, if you throw out that outlier and look at all the other places you can land as a business.
summary: The venture-funded unicorn path has an astronomically low success rate; there's far more room to build successful businesses outside that narrow model.

speaker: Jason Fried
text: Hopefully, I don't come off as encouraging everyone to be like me. I'm not saying that at all. What I'm saying is, this is a way to be. It's an alternative to what you're often hearing in our industry, which is, 'Go big or go home.'
summary: Jason Fried presents bootstrapping not as the only way but as a legitimate alternative to the dominant Silicon Valley narrative of go-big-or-go-home.

speaker: Matthew Dicks
text: Everyone loves the word storytelling in business. It's a huge buzzword. They love to think of themselves as storytellers, but when they come to me, they don't really want to be storytellers. Because to be a storyteller means you have to separate yourself from the herd, and in their mind, that risks them getting picked off.
summary: Business professionals want the label of storyteller without the vulnerability it requires -- true storytelling means standing out from the herd.

speaker: Matthew Dicks
text: How many times have you gone to a conference, listened to someone speak, and by the time you're pulling into the driveway, you really can't remember anything that they said? Because that's what happens if we don't speak in story. Our minds are not designed to remember a pie chart or facts or statistics or platitudes or ideas that are not attached to imagery.
summary: Without story, presentations are forgotten by the drive home because human brains are not wired to retain isolated facts, charts, or platitudes.

speaker: Matthew Dicks
text: The risk you take if you're not telling stories is that you will be forgotten. 100%. You will be forgotten.
summary: The biggest risk in communication is not vulnerability from storytelling but the certainty of being forgotten without it.

speaker: Katie Dill
text: Katie Dill shares her story of failure from the compilation episode on the overlooked wisdom that comes from product failures and personal setbacks.
summary: The failure compilation episode gathers the most insightful stories of failure from over 100 podcast episodes to extract overlooked wisdom.

speaker: Various Guests
text: From freezing onstage in front of thousands of people, to coworkers staging an intervention, to huge product investments that went to zero, we've pulled our favorite stories of failure from 100+ podcast episodes.
summary: The episode compiles raw failure stories ranging from public humiliation to billion-dollar product failures, normalizing setbacks as a source of wisdom.

speaker: Various Guests
text: You can learn a lot and often a lot more from failure.
summary: Studying how the best companies fail often teaches more than studying how they succeed.

speaker: Kim Scott
text: If you say, 'Do you have any feedback for me?' You're wasting your breath. The other person's going to say, 'Oh no, everything's fine.' The question that I like to ask is, 'What could I do or stop doing that would make it easier to work with me?'
summary: Generic feedback requests fail because they're too easy to dodge; asking what to do or stop doing that would make working together easier forces a real answer.

speaker: Kim Scott
text: Do not write down my question because if you sound like Kim Scott and not like yourself, then other people are not going to believe you want the answer. It needs to sound authentic to you.
summary: Feedback questions must be phrased in your own authentic voice, not copied from a book, or people will not believe you genuinely want the answer.

speaker: Kim Scott
text: If everybody can write down their question, who they're going to ask it of and then pop it into their calendar right now, this will be one of the most productive podcasts in all of podcast land.
summary: Kim Scott challenges listeners to immediately schedule asking their personalized feedback question to someone specific, turning insight into action.

speaker: Anuj Rathi
text: There are only three reasons why things do not happen the way you want them to happen as a leader. Either that person can't do, which is a capability issue, or they won't do, which is a motivation or an alignment issue, or they were not set up to do, which is really your problem that you didn't set up the ways of working properly.
summary: When things go wrong as a leader, the root cause is always one of three things: capability gap, motivation gap, or a systemic setup failure on your part.

speaker: Anuj Rathi
text: If they won't do, why won't they? Are they not aligned to you? Do they not agree with your vision? Do they not just have enough time? You need to really go deeper there.
summary: When someone 'won't do' the work, leaders must dig deeper to understand whether it's misalignment, disagreement with the vision, or simply a capacity issue.

speaker: Anuj Rathi
text: As a leader, do you have the right people in terms of capability? If not, is the right answer for us to coach them or to really put them... or mentor them, or move them to some other place because maybe their capability is suited elsewhere?
summary: A full-stack PM leader must constantly assess whether to coach, mentor, or redeploy people based on where their capabilities are best suited.

speaker: Ebi Atawodi
text: I do not believe in being liked. I believe in being loved. And love is the choice to extend yourself for the spiritual growth of oneself or another. When you are extending yourself, you're not nice. It sometimes is having hard conversations.
summary: True leadership is about being loved, not liked, which means extending yourself for others' growth even when it requires uncomfortable honesty.

speaker: Ebi Atawodi
text: It's knowing that, oh, there's a human, they know I care about them. So when the feedback is coming like raw, they know that it's in their best interest because I've shown enough times that I genuinely care about the person behind the role.
summary: Raw, honest feedback is only received well when you've built a foundation of genuine care for the person behind the professional role.

speaker: Ebi Atawodi
text: And that's love. And when you are extending yourself, you're not nice. It's not always nice or like, it sometimes is having hard conversations.
summary: Caring deeply about someone's growth often looks like having difficult conversations rather than being pleasant and agreeable.

speaker: Inbal Shani
text: The user of the AI tools to develop software needs to form a different thinking. You need to start figuring out how are you using these AI tools to help you be successful. And it's no longer just the actual code writing, it's really evolving your thinking to the big picture, to the connected experience, to connected systems.
summary: AI coding tools require developers to shift from code-writing thinking to systems-level thinking about connected experiences and architecture.

speaker: Inbal Shani
text: The junior developers, when they start, usually we expect them to be able to write simple code. But if now there is an AI system that is helping them writing code, they can spend more time from the get-go understanding the system, understanding the environment that they're building, or understanding that product that they're building, which today they don't have time because they're still learning how to code.
summary: AI coding tools could leapfrog junior developers past basic code-writing to focus on understanding systems and products from their first day.

speaker: Inbal Shani
text: Which today we just find it more in the world of more senior developers and less and less in the junior developers.
summary: Systems-level thinking has traditionally been a senior developer skill, but AI tools may make it accessible to developers at all levels.

speaker: Shishir Mehrotra
text: A group of scientists have invented a teleportation device. They've hired you to be their business counterpart, bring this to market. You could be a product manager for this thing. What do you do? Usually people will start asking a bunch of questions. At some point I'll say, they will answer only two of your questions. What two questions do you ask?
summary: Shishir Mehrotra's teleportation device interview question tests whether candidates can identify the few critical questions that drive all downstream decisions.

speaker: Eeke De Miliano
text: To what do you attribute your success? And you can't say luck. Because I think humble people will always say luck in some way, and I always kind of want to know, how self-aware are you, basically, and how curious are you?
summary: Asking candidates to explain their success without citing luck reveals their self-awareness, curiosity, and ability to reflect on what actually drove their achievements.

speaker: Nikhyl Singhal
text: What's something that everyone takes for granted that you think is essentially hogwash or inaccurate? I'm always looking for people to break this sort of interview mindset. There's no way to answer that question without being genuinely opinionated.
summary: Asking candidates what conventional wisdom they think is wrong forces authenticity and breaks through the rehearsed interview performance.

speaker: Paul Adams
text: What feedback will I be giving this person in their first performance review? It's an amazing question, because the person can't dodge it. There's an answer, and it's incredibly enlightening.
summary: The best reference call question is asking what feedback you'll give in their first review, because it's impossible to dodge and reveals genuine development areas.

speaker: Tom Conrad
text: There's this belief that everybody needs to be a founder. I think, in some ways, our industry would be much better off if there were fewer founders. There's an entire category of smart, creative, hardworking, talented, borderline visionary people who can raise that $2 million seed and go off and build some stupid company that's never going to go anywhere.
summary: The tech industry's founder worship wastes talented people on doomed startups when they'd create more impact joining existing teams with real traction.

speaker: Tom Conrad
text: That would be so much better off finding a team that needs their skillset and working on a problem that has a mathematical formula that's going to win on any metric. Whatever metric you care about. You want the acclaim of your peers, you want financial reward, you want outside impact on culture, whatever the thing is that gets you out of bed every morning, you can achieve that in collaboration with others.
summary: The best career outcomes often come from joining the right team rather than founding your own company, regardless of what metric of success you care about.

speaker: Tom Conrad
text: You don't have to be the person that raises the seed round.
summary: Being a founder is not the only path to outsized impact, financial reward, or fulfillment in tech.

speaker: Paul Millerd
text: Take three hours during a workday. Has to be during a workday. Block off your schedule, sneak out. Go for a walk without a destination or do something from your childhood that you used to do all the time. And just pay attention. What is emerging? Do you feel bad for sneaking out of work? Where does that bad feeling come from?
summary: Taking three hours off during a workday to do something unstructured reveals your deepest assumptions about work, identity, and self-worth.

speaker: Paul Millerd
text: A lot of people have never really thought about, why do you work? People say money. Okay, that's fine. But what else? Why? Are you trying to be a good person? Do you see a good person as somebody that works every day? Maybe. These are a lot of scripts people grow up with.
summary: Most people have never examined why they work beyond money, operating on unquestioned scripts about work equaling virtue that were inherited from childhood.

speaker: Paul Millerd
text: You're really just creating this space to get in touch with, how do I actually feel about work? How do I get to know myself more? Are there things that I've lost touch with that really bring me alive?
summary: Creating space away from work is not about laziness but about reconnecting with what genuinely energizes you beyond your professional identity.

speaker: Ramesh Johari
text: Marketplaces are a little bit like a game of whac-a-mole. We decided to build some custom bespoke features that were really going to direct new supply to more experienced folks on the other side of the market. Good. And then lo and behold, now the existing folks on the other side are having a worse experience.
summary: Marketplace management is a constant whac-a-mole game where improving one side's experience inevitably degrades the other side's metrics.

speaker: Ramesh Johari
text: A lot of marketplace management is moving attention and inventory around. Many of the changes that are most consequential create winners and losers. And rolling with those changes is about recognizing whether the winners you've created are more important to your business than the losers you've created in the process.
summary: The hardest marketplace decisions involve accepting that consequential changes always create losers, and judging whether the winners matter more to your business.

speaker: Ramesh Johari
text: Your metrics just keep moving around. And that's because the whac-a-mole game here is ultimately about moving attention and inventory around.
summary: Marketplace metrics constantly shift because every intervention redistributes attention and inventory, creating new problems as it solves old ones.

speaker: Maggie Crowley
text: If you ever find yourself saying something like, that's not my job, that's probably a thing you should do. You can spend your life getting frustrated at that or you can just get over and get the work done. And people who are willing to just get the work done will move faster.
summary: When a PM thinks 'that's not my job,' that's usually the exact thing they should do, because ownership over unglamorous work is what separates great PMs.

speaker: Maggie Crowley
text: As a PM, for better or for worse, you're oftentimes the emotional center of the team and it's your job to keep people motivated, keep people excited, keep them bought into the project, and you just have to keep that optimism going and it's hard work.
summary: PMs are the emotional center of their team, responsible for maintaining motivation and optimism even when the work is unglamorous.

speaker: Maggie Crowley
text: Their products will be more successful and they probably aren't carrying around all that anger and crappy emotion because... you just have to do whatever it takes.
summary: PMs who embrace a 'whatever it takes' mentality not only ship more successful products but also feel less frustrated than those guarding their job boundaries.

speaker: Bill Carr
text: Jeff would say, we took it as an article of faith. If we served customers well, if we prioritized customers and delivered for them, things like sales, things like revenue and active customers and things like the share price and free cash flow would follow.
summary: Amazon's foundational belief was that obsessing over customer outcomes would cause revenue, stock price, and free cash flow to follow as natural consequences.

speaker: Bill Carr
text: When we're making a decision thinking about a problem, we're going to start with what's best for the customer and then come backward from there. That informs what's the work you have to do to then create this new solution for customers.
summary: Amazon's 'working backwards' method starts every decision with what's best for the customer and reverse-engineers the required work from there.

speaker: Bill Carr
text: So therefore, when we're making a decision thinking about a problem, we're going to start with what's best for the customer and then come backward from there.
summary: The 'working backwards' philosophy makes customer benefit the starting point of every product decision rather than business metrics.

speaker: Eric Ries
text: People act like having a startup fail is the worst thing that can happen to you. And man, that's not even in the top 10. Far worse is to be in a company that won't die, a zombie, undead company that you hate, but you can't leave.
summary: A clean startup failure is far less damaging than being trapped in a zombie company that won't die but that you hate -- the psychological toll of the latter is much worse.

speaker: Eric Ries
text: We're having a mental health crisis among founders that's not talked about enough. When you build a company and you sell it out and it becomes something that you find abhorrent, man, maybe you get rich, but it's not good.
summary: The untold founder mental health crisis comes not from failure but from success that creates something the founder finds morally abhorrent.

speaker: Eric Ries
text: Building a company you hate, that becomes a maligned force in the world, that you have to go pretend you weren't involved with or like you feel complicit in... That's way, way worse, and I wish more founders would take that more seriously early on.
summary: Eric Ries urges founders to take seriously the risk of building something that succeeds but becomes a force they're ashamed to be associated with.

speaker: Paul Adams
text: This is a meteor coming towards you. This is going to radically transform society. And I think if people don't explore AI properly, it will leave them behind.
summary: Paul Adams frames AI as an extinction-level event for unprepared products, urging teams to treat it with the urgency of a meteor heading toward them.

speaker: Paul Adams
text: I'd start with the thing your product does. 'What's the core premise behind it? Why do people use it? What problem does it solve for them?' Go back to basics. And then ask, 'Can AI do that?' And for a lot, the answer is going to be, 'Yes, it can.'
summary: Intercom's CPO recommends starting your AI strategy by asking whether AI can do the core thing your product does, and many will find the answer is yes.

speaker: Paul Adams
text: For some it'll be replacement, AI would replace, it'll just do it. And, in other places, it'll be augmentation. It'll augment. It'll help people. But yeah, I think that you've got to match your product, and what AI can do, and what it will be able to do, and then ask yourself, 'Okay, what are we going to do?'
summary: AI will either replace or augment different parts of every product, and companies must map their features against AI capabilities to decide their response.

speaker: Gina Gotthilf
text: Communication is constantly underrated. And communication isn't about being able to convey a message, it's about being able to convey a message in a way that the listener receives it, and understands it, and remembers it. And that's really hard to do.
summary: Effective communication is not about sending messages but about ensuring they are received, understood, and remembered, which is a fundamentally different skill.

speaker: Gina Gotthilf
text: One of the things I've helped employ at Duolingo is a unique voice. There is always a quirk, like it's unexpected. The way we talk to you is a little bit funny. It doesn't take ourselves too seriously and it makes the person receiving this message feel something.
summary: Duolingo's distinctive brand voice works because it makes users feel something through humor and unexpectedness rather than generic corporate messaging.

speaker: Gina Gotthilf
text: Again, it's about how you make people feel. And you feel like either you giggle, or you're like, wait what? They just did what? And using that to your benefit.
summary: The secret to Duolingo's growth was making people feel something, whether through laughter or surprise, creating organic sharing.

speaker: Katie Dill
text: The use of the word beauty in books that have been digitized by Google has decreased, like pretty dramatically. And it's aligned with this idea of, 'Well, functionality is king.' As if people think about functionality and beauty as like two opposite things. No, they're not two opposite things. Beauty enhances functionality.
summary: The false dichotomy between beauty and functionality has caused a measurable decline in valuing aesthetics, when in fact beauty directly enhances usability.

speaker: Katie Dill
text: Things that are more beautiful, increase trust. You see that we've put painstaking detail into this, and we care about the details of how something works, and that gives you assurance that we care about other details that you can't see too.
summary: Beautiful design builds trust by signaling that a company cares about visible details, which implies they also care about the invisible ones.

speaker: Katie Dill
text: The other piece of it that is not talked about in business as often is just the importance of how people feel. Things that are more beautiful, increase trust.
summary: Business undervalues how products make people feel, yet beauty is one of the most powerful drivers of user trust and product adoption.

speaker: Jason Feifer
text: The editor, the writer, I'll just say it as plainly as possible. They don't care about you. They don't care about you. They care about their reader or their listener or their viewer. That's who they care about.
summary: Media gatekeepers serve their audience, not you, so approach them by understanding what value you bring to their readers.

speaker: Jason Feifer
text: Before you ever pitch, just get me to recognize your name. Just engage in social media in a very casual way, such that when you email me, I think, do I know who that person is? It just makes it more likely that I'll open the email.
summary: Building name recognition through casual social media engagement before pitching dramatically increases your chances of getting a response from editors.

speaker: Jason Feifer
text: Sometimes you are not the story, but you can be part of the story. Sometimes that means you could just be a writer. You might try to just pitch authoritative articles by you to different publications.
summary: If your company is hard to write about directly, position yourself as part of a bigger trend or offer expert commentary to get press indirectly.

speaker: Jason Feifer
text: Opportunity set B is what's available to you, even though nobody's asking you to do it. What I have found throughout my own career is that opportunity set B is always more important, infinitely more important. Opportunity set A, everything that's asked of you. Opportunity set B is where growth happens.
summary: Career growth comes not from excelling at what is asked of you, but from pursuing opportunities that are available to you that nobody is requesting.

speaker: Karri Saarinen
text: The more it matters, the more the design matters. Whenever there's a new paradigm, the first iterations of those products don't have to be super well designed. Then, as you build the 100,000 different email clients, any email client now has to be pretty good to be even considered.
summary: As product categories mature, the bar for design quality rises dramatically and becomes table stakes for being taken seriously.

speaker: Karri Saarinen
text: Today it's almost a very basic thing now. Pretty much from the very beginning, you need pretty high level design for people to even pay attention or consider you seriously.
summary: In today's saturated market, high-quality design is no longer a differentiator but a baseline requirement to earn user attention.
